Case study

Design Strategy

Role

Design Strategist
User Researcher

Company

Moody's Analytics
Fund Finance Ratings

Timeline

2025, 3 months
to MVP launch

Impact

34 users, 91 facilities,
1000+ activities logged

TLDR

Joined a 0→1 product with three months to MVP, no handoff, and a team that had lost analyst trust. I restructured how design, product, and process worked together. We shipped on time.

The situation

A new product, a broken process, and three months to launch.

Private Credit was a new line of business at Moody's, announced in late 2025. When I joined as lead designer, the MVP was three months out and there was no formal handoff from the previous designer. What I walked into had five compounding problems.

1
No established business process
Business practices for Private Credit were still being developed in parallel with the tool, meaning there was no stable foundation to design against.
2
Requirements getting lost
Analysts told the process team, who told product, who told design. By the time requirements reached me they were misrepresented or missing entirely.
3
SMEs losing trust
SMEs couldn't see their feedback reflected in designs. They began questioning whether working with the product team was worthwhile at all.
4
Design built on assumptions
The existing tool had been designed without direct analyst input. Core workflows weren't supported, and the product team had no way to discover this without going directly to users.
5
A hard deadline with no runway
Three months to MVP. No handoff from the previous designer. No grace period to get up to speed. Every day spent untangling the situation was a day not spent designing.

Plan of action

Three things had to happen in parallel.

I quickly assessed what was blocking us and focused on three tracks: understand what analysts actually needed right now, rebuild SME confidence through visibility, and fix how information flowed between teams.

1
Identify immediate need
Analysts were clear the existing design didn't reflect their requirements. Before anything else, I needed to understand what they actually needed.
2
Regain SME confidence
SMEs had started questioning whether collaborating with the product team was worth their time. Rebuilding that trust was a prerequisite to getting anything done.
3
Change the pipeline
Requirements were getting lost across too many layers. I needed a more direct, reliable way to gather and align on what needed to be built.

Research

I spent time in meetings with analysts, product, and process, separately and then together.

To understand what each team was actually saying, I joined their syncs individually before trying to align them. I spent the first two weeks in meetings, taking notes, and mapping where requirements were getting lost in translation. Three layers of communication (analyst to process to product to design) meant that by the time a requirement reached me, it had often been reinterpreted or dropped entirely.

It quickly became clear that analysts needed a way to upload documents and create covenants for each facility they were monitoring — neither of which the existing tool supported, despite both needs having been raised multiple times.

Notes from cross-team syncs, the raw material for aligning on requirements

I then did a gap analysis of the existing design to map exactly what was missing. The findings were concrete: no way to monitor covenants, no separation between global and periodic data, no way to assign individual due dates, no way to organize uploaded documents.

Gap analysis — existing design annotated with what was missing

Design

Rather than designing in isolation, I brought SMEs into the process directly.

Given how much had been lost in translation before I joined, I wanted SMEs to see their requirements reflected in designs as they evolved, not after decisions had already been made. I ran two collaborative design sessions, walking through early explorations together and pressure-testing assumptions in real time.

Iterating in Figma during collaborative sessions with SME champions

After two sessions, we reached sign-off on the design direction and I moved into higher fidelity. The key additions: covenant tracking as an MVP feature, separation between global and periodic data, the ability to track data across multiple periods, categorized document upload, and AI-assisted data extraction.

Annotated new design directions, documenting how each identified gap was resolved

Process change

Fixing the product wasn't enough. The way the team worked together had to change too.

In parallel with the design work, I recognized that the information pipeline was a structural problem, not a one-off fix. Requirements would keep getting distorted unless the process changed. I used AI to help draft a proposal for a new, more direct way of collaborating across design, product, process, and SMEs.

The one-page operating model, adopted by the full team

The anchor of the new model was a standing weekly design session that SME champions were required to attend. Requirements would be defined, designs would evolve in real time, and decisions would be made on the spot rather than relayed through layers of communication. I also advocated for a separate pool of analyst participants, independent of the SME working group, that I could draw from for research sessions as needs arose.

Final designs

An MVP that actually reflected what analysts needed.

Final designs — Covenants tracker and Fund Details document upload

Outcome

Launched on time, to real users, with real adoption.

The Private Credit Digital Tool launched its MVP on time, which, given the situation we started from, was the most meaningful measure of success. In a presentation to senior leadership three months after launch, the team reported adoption numbers that validated both the design decisions and the new operating model.

34
users onboarded at launch
91
facilities created in the first reporting period (each representing a portfolio of loans actively monitored by analysts)
1k+
monitoring activities logged

If I were doing this again, I'd push harder to get direct analyst access earlier. Even one or two 30-minute sessions in week one would have accelerated the gap analysis significantly. The SME champions were invaluable, but they were representing analyst needs secondhand. The moments where we got the most useful signal were always when we went directly to end users.
